results.title: When AI Agents Say “Done,” Ask for Proof
results.content:
That is where the real problem begins. When an agent finishes a market research brief, a customer list cleanup, a web-scraping task, or a file-organization workflow, what proof do you have that the work was actually done properly? Did it check the right sources? Did it skip a page because it timed out? Did it rely on an outdated article? Did it retry a failed tool call, or did it silently move on and fill the gap with a plausible conclusion?

As agents move from answering questions to operating tools, the old way of judging AI output starts to break. A final answer is no longer enough. If an agent can browse, read, write, modify, send, delete, or submit, then users need more than a result. They need a trace of the work.

## The old productivity metric is broken [...] A web search can return stale results. A file parser can miss a table. A CRM update can partially succeed. A browser task can time out after changing one field but before saving another. A spreadsheet operation can apply the right formula to the wrong range. A retrieval step can find relevant but outdated context.

None of these failures necessarily produce an obvious error.

The final output may still look polished.

That is why I think the next serious agent feature is not “more autonomy.” It is proof of work.

Not proof in the blockchain sense. Proof in the everyday workplace sense:

Show me what you touched.

Show me what you checked.

Show me what failed.

Show me what you assumed.

Show me what still needs human review.

## The trust problem is operational, not emotional [...] I first started thinking about this while watching people hand more real work to agents: competitor research, customer list cleanup, spreadsheet updates, web scraping, weekly reports, CRM enrichment, file organization. The agent disappears into a workflow, comes back later, and says: “Done.”

But what does “done” mean?

Did it check the right websites? Did it skip a source because the page timed out? Did it use an outdated article? Did it retry a failed tool call? Did it quietly make up a reasonable-looking conclusion when the evidence was thin?

That is the new trust problem in agentic AI:

The result is not enough. The process must be visible.

## The old productivity metric is broken

For years, productivity software was judged by three simple questions.

Is it faster?

Is it cheaper?

results.title: ICLR 2026: 21% of Peer Reviews Are AI-Generated
results.content:
## Impact on Researchers

### Frustration with AI-generated reviews

For many researchers, the Pangram analysis confirmed their worst suspicions. Desmond Elliott, a computer scientist at the University of Copenhagen, received three reviews for his submission, and one seemed to have missed "the point of the paper."

His PhD student who led the work suspected the review was generated by LLMs because it:

When Pangram released its findings, Elliott immediately checked his paper's reviews. The suspect review, which Pangram flagged as fully AI-generated, had given the manuscript the lowest rating, leaving it "on the borderline between accept and reject."

"It's deeply frustrating," Elliott said, highlighting the real-world impact of AI-generated reviews on researchers' work and careers.

#### Discussion about this post

H. Floyd

Apr 28

The CFO wire example is the one I would be nervous about in a real rollout.

The agent is not broken in the obvious sense. It is doing something it was allowed to do. The problem is that the business needs a way to narrow that permission while the run is still alive.

That is where I think the kill-switch conversation gets more interesting.
